guest_info.name: John Carmack
guest_info.title: Cofounder of id Software, CTO of Oculus VR

summary: John Carmack discusses his early programming experiences, the development of groundbreaking games like Doom and Quake, his work at Oculus VR, and his current focus on artificial general intelligence. He also delves into programming languages, software development methodologies, and the challenges of building the metaverse.

main_entities.name: id Software
main_entities.context: Game development company cofounded by John Carmack, responsible for Commander Keen, Wolfenstein 3D, Doom, and Quake.

main_entities.name: Doom
main_entities.context: Revolutionary first-person shooter game developed by id Software, known for its technology and impact on gaming culture.

main_entities.name: Quake
main_entities.context: Another groundbreaking game by id Software, known for its true 3D engine and modding capabilities.

main_entities.name: Wolfenstein 3D
main_entities.context: Early first-person shooter game that popularized the genre, developed by id Software.

main_entities.name: Oculus VR
main_entities.context: Virtual reality company where John Carmack served as CTO, contributing to the development of VR technology and the metaverse.

main_entities.name: C++
main_entities.context: Programming language favored by John Carmack for serious programming, praised for its control and flexibility.

main_entities.name: Python
main_entities.context: Programming language used by John Carmack for AI and ML work, noted for its convenience but potential inefficiencies.

main_entities.name: Shareware
main_entities.context: Software distribution model popularized by Apogee, where a portion of the game is free and the rest is paid for.

main_entities.name: Ray casting
main_entities.context: Rendering technique used in Wolfenstein 3D to create a pseudo-3D environment.

main_entities.name: Binary Space Partitioning (BSP)
main_entities.context: Technique used in Doom and Quake to efficiently render 3D environments by dividing space into smaller partitions.

main_entities.name: Metaverse
main_entities.context: Interconnected virtual worlds, a concept John Carmack has been involved with through his work at Oculus VR and his reflections on earlier games.

main_entities.name: OpenGL
main_entities.context: Graphics API that John Carmack championed, which became crucial for mobile graphics acceleration.

main_entities.name: Assembly Language
main_entities.context: Low-level programming language used by John Carmack in his early game development for performance optimization.

main_entities.name: Commander Keen
main_entities.context: Early game developed by id Software, showcasing scrolling technology and shareware distribution.

key_details.fact: John Carmack's first program was '10 print John Carmack' on a TRS-80 computer.
Early game design was heavily limited by the capabilities of the computer hardware.
John Carmack considers the best programming language to be the one that works and is currently being used.
The .plan file was used in the early days of id Software as a proto-blog to share work logs and thoughts.
Wolfenstein 3D used ray casting and compiled scalers to create a pseudo-3D environment.
Doom was designed to be modified by the user community through WAD files.
John Carmack believes that the metaverse should be built by creating something amazing that people love and expanding its capabilities.
Beat Saber is a successful VR game because it plays to the strengths of VR and hides its weaknesses.
John Carmack pushed Romero out of id Software due to differences in work ethic and vision.
John Carmack believes that the hacker ethic is about sharing information and taking joy in other people's accomplishments.

other_persons_mentioned.name: John Romero
other_persons_mentioned.context: Cofounder of id Software, known for his design and artistic contributions to their games.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Tom Hall
other_persons_mentioned.context: Creative mind behind Commander Keen and contributor to early id Software games.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Jay Wilbur
other_persons_mentioned.context: Editor at Softdisk who helped John Carmack get his start.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Scott Miller
other_persons_mentioned.context: Founder of Apogee Software, who pioneered the shareware distribution model.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Michael Abrash
other_persons_mentioned.context: Assembly language expert hired by id Software to optimize Quake.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Ken Silverman
other_persons_mentioned.context: Creator of the Build engine, which competed with Doom's engine.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Neal Stephenson
other_persons_mentioned.context: Author of Snow Crash, the book that coined the term 'metaverse'.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Mark Zuckerberg
other_persons_mentioned.context: CEO of Meta, company that acquired Oculus VR.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Joe Rogan
other_persons_mentioned.context: Talk show host and friend, who is a big fan of Quake.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Elon Musk
other_persons_mentioned.context: Founder of SpaceX, discussed in relation to programming language choices.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Boz
other_persons_mentioned.context: Facebook executive, discussed in relation to hiring practices.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Graham Devine
other_persons_mentioned.context: Former programmer at id Software and Seventh Guest.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Adrian
other_persons_mentioned.context: Artist who worked on early id Software games.
